[134:14]And here's the thing about tariffs.

[134:17]And this is the problem.

[134:23]I've said this really for the past 10 years, ever since Trump got elected the first time.

[134:28]The problem with how Trump views tariffs, or I should say maybe people in Trump's cabinet,

[134:36]the problem with how they view tariffs, is that they are effectively free traders, which

[134:50]To the extent that they want to change our trade policy,

[134:54]they want the United States to export to other countries.

[134:58]They want to reduce trade deficits and get us to export to other countries.

[135:02]Some of them don't even believe in that.

[135:05]Many of them only view tariffs as a tool of foreign policy.

[135:11]Access to the markets inside the United States

[135:16]is extremely powerful and it makes other countries rich.

[135:20]We gave them access to the very rich American market. And by selectively taking that away,

[135:27]that is a form of soft power. So for example, earlier in the year, Trump imposed tariffs on

[135:33]Mexico and Canada. He said, because he wanted to, he wanted them to do a better job policing their

[135:44]And so Trump said, you cannot have access to our markets.

[135:48]This would destroy both of their economies.

[135:50]This would destroy the businesses that employ so many people there and are a big source

[135:55]of their wealth.

[135:57]He said, we will basically destroy and cripple your economy.

[136:00]It's over for you if you don't make key concessions.

[136:12]That's using them as a tool of statecraft.

[136:15]The State Department is negotiating with the foreign ministries of Canada and Mexico,

[136:19]and they're using tariffs to coerce those governments to do something that's unrelated

[136:25]to trade.

[136:27]Immigration and fentanyl have really nothing to do with trade at all.

[136:30]They have nothing to do with our economy.

[136:32]And unfortunately, all too often, that is the only way, that's the only reason that

[136:42]We're going to put tariffs on Canada to get them to do this, put tariffs on Mexico to

[136:47]get them to do this, put sanctions on Iran or the secondary sanctions with Venezuela,

[136:54]25% tariff on anyone that does business with Venezuela for a foreign policy objective.

[137:01]But the problem is that we need tariffs for the economy.

[137:08]You can threaten tariffs, and maybe they go on and maybe they don't, or maybe they go

[137:12]on until the country does what we want them to do, and then they're taken away.

[137:17]We need tariffs to be a part of our economy.

[137:19]They need to be in the mix basically permanently.

[137:25]For the purpose of building the American economy, not to get Mexico to do X, Y, and Z, not to

[137:31]get Venezuela or its allies to do something, we need tariffs to build our own economy.

[137:38]And in order to do that, tariffs need to be implemented in a very specific way.

[137:44]Right now, there is a report.

[137:54]And it says that consumer spending in the first quarter of this year indicates that

[138:28]you have another month. Maybe I'll do tariffs everywhere. Actually, maybe not everywhere,

[138:33]but maybe I will, but maybe not. When they are unpredictable, companies and investors

[138:46]If the United States is going to apply a 25% tariff to everything coming across its

[138:52]borders from Canada, obviously companies are not going to invest if they think that that

[138:59]will affect them.

[139:00]It's going to change their decision making, whether that does or does not happen.

[139:04]So they hang onto their money and they wait to see what happens.

[139:08]And that's one example, but that happens every time there's a big tariff threat.

[139:12]And that's actually bad for the economy to have that uncertainty.

[139:17]The way that you impose tariffs, if you want them to be a part of your economic policy

[139:23]for the long term, if you actually want companies or countries or anybody to invest in the United

[139:31]States, they need to be predictable.

[139:35]You need to tell them what the rates are going to be, what goods you're going to affect,

[139:40]when they're going to come into effect, give a reasonable amount of time, and then you

[139:45]have to follow through.

[139:46]And if they're implemented in such a way, where there's transparency, where they are

[139:51]telegraphed in advance, where there is time to coordinate economic activity.

[139:56]And when they are predictable,

[139:58]you don't have as much volatility in the markets.

[140:01]Then people can plan and they can prepare.

[140:07]and people have their own theories

[145:32]You know, let's say that they're putting up the tariffs

[145:36]And they say it's for foreign policy. I'm okay with them saying that, but they need

[145:59]but not if Canada does this. Maybe we'll put across the board and never mind.

[146:14]And here's this last thing I'll say about it, then we'll move on.

[146:19]And I explained this on a Twitter space not too long ago, and in my debate with Dean Withers, they all talk about, but tariffs make things more expensive.

[146:27]Tariffs are going to cause spending to go, or rather prices to go up.

[146:33]Here's the thing about tariffs, even to the extent that you talk about tariffs as a tool

[146:39]for foreign policy.

[146:43]Productive capacity is the foundation of everything.

[146:47]And I'm using that word very specifically.

[146:51]People talk about the economy.

[146:53]What does the economy mean?

[146:58]We export our debt, we export our dollars.

[147:01]Everybody denominates their trade in dollars.

[147:04]They need our currency, they need the paper.

[147:07]That's not the economy.

[147:08]The GDP, the earnings reports, the stock market,

[147:12]that's not the economy.

[147:13]The economy is the actual stuff.

[147:16]Capital used to mean machines.

[147:20]When Karl Marx and Ricardo and Adam Smith

[147:23]talked about capital, they meant machines,

[147:25]they meant factories.

[147:27]They meant actual stuff.

[147:30]That's what the economy really is.

[147:33]When you look at China and they make cars, when you look at Japan and they make cars,

[147:37]when you look at China and they make everything, that's economy.

[147:41]That's capital.

[147:42]It's productive capacity.

[147:46]So people talk a lot about, we need to grow the economy.

[147:49]We need the economy to be bigger.

[147:50]We need to be economically competitive.

[147:56]The foundation of everything is not the economy, as evidenced by our war in Ukraine with Russia,

[148:02]because you can't send money to go and fight Russians.

[148:09]You need artillery shells.

[148:11]You can't use money to heat your homes in the winter or power your factories.

[148:16]You need natural gas that comes from pipelines, that comes from the ground.

[148:21]And that's why Russia is winning the war over Europe.

[148:25]Because for all of the economy that Europe

[148:28]and the United States has, Russia can make more shells.

[148:31]Russia has natural gas, Russia has fertilizer.

[148:35]Russia has all these things and they can trade with China

[148:37]and they could get the things they don't have

[148:39]or the things that they don't make.

[148:40]That's economy.

[148:42]The productive capacity of a nation

[148:44]is the foundation of everything.

[148:46]If you don't have productive capacity, you don't have power.

[148:50]You don't have military strength.

[148:53]You can't control the seas.

[148:55]You can't, and then, by the way,

[148:58]if you don't have the productive capacity to make ships,

[149:01]to make aircraft carriers, to make face masks

[149:04]during the pandemic, to make medical equipment,

[149:07]to make chips, to make computers,

[149:10]if you don't have the productive capacity

[149:12]to make the things that you need,

[149:14]to project power in the world.

[149:17]If you don't have power projection in the world,

[149:19]guess who controls trade?

[149:22]The country that does have power,

[149:24]the country that does have productive capacity.

[149:27]And so, you know, there's sort of this irony

[149:29]where people say like,

[149:32]You know, we're going to use tariffs for foreign policy. We don't really care. You know, we're going to have free trade and we're not really going to pursue a solid industrial policy.

[149:41]We're going to use tariffs to get the people to overthrow Venezuela.

[149:46]It's like, well, we're playing games doing that. China is building warships. So we're playing games with that. They're building the industries of the future.

[149:54]It's all in-house. The supply chains are controlled by them.

[149:58]Who will have the ability to project power in 50 years and then who will control the global economy? Then who will control anything?

[150:06]So you have to get these things right now. This is fundamental. This is extremely important. This should be a priority instead of using tariffs to

[150:15]Secure a short-term concession. We should have tariffs in place comprehensively and permanently

[150:22]so that we can, in the long term,

[150:25]rebuild our productive capability,

[150:27]our productive capacity as a country.

[150:30]That is something that resonated with me

[150:32]in the first Trump administration,

[150:34]or in the first Trump campaign, I should say,

[150:36]but it never got off the ground.

[150:39]It has been 11 years now,

[150:43]or I should say nine years,

[150:45]since Trump won the election the first time.

[150:48]We're hardly better off.

[150:49]We have some tariffs on aluminum.

[150:52]On steel, we have the tariffs on China, we're put in place in the first Trump admin, stayed

[150:58]there through Biden, are there now?

[151:01]But 10 years is a long time for there to not be any kind of whole of government approach

[151:10]So this is my concern, and I'm a little more optimistic about this than I am about immigration

[151:49]Today, it was reported in the Atlantic that if you remember a couple weeks ago, I guess it was last week,

[151:56]the Trump administration

[151:58]restarted the US war in Yemen.

[152:02]So last year during the Biden administration, the United States and the United Kingdom

[152:06]were a war with the Houthi rebels in northern Yemen.

[152:12]They're attacking shipping in the Red Sea and we're bombing them. We're bombing now their ports, their city and

[152:20]Trump has

[152:21]Restarted the war as of last week hit him with major airstrikes. They've been going on almost every day since last week

[152:30]It's far more significant than anything that happened last year and what we've learned today

[152:36]is that before the strikes began,

[152:39]these are very significant, very consequential strikes.

[152:42]This is a serious military action.

[152:45]This is the most serious military action

[152:47]since Trump took office.

[152:49]We have now learned that before the strikes began,

[152:53]a group chat was created on Signal,

[152:56]the encrypted messaging app,

[152:59]which included the vice president,

[153:02]national security advisor, secretary of defense,

[153:04]secretary of state.

[153:06]And apparently they accidentally added to the group chat

[153:11]The editor-in-chief of the Atlantic the newspaper

[153:21]So they were discussing the targets. They were discussing which weapons they would use

[153:26]They were discussing the timing of the strike

[153:30]Which cities they were going to hit?

[153:33]This is apparently the highest level

[153:36]talk between the critical elements in our military industrial complex or the I should

[153:43]say State Department Defense Department planning out this strike, and they accidentally had

[153:50]a journalist eavesdropping and reading the entire conversation.

[153:54]It was the journalist, by the way, who discovered that this happened.

[154:01]They didn't know that they added him on accident.

[154:04]He left and then informed the public.

[154:07]They didn't even know the entire time.

[154:09]It's not like in other words, he was sitting there

[154:12]and then Rubio said, hey, wait a second.

[154:16]I don't recognize that number.

[154:18]And they removed him.

[154:20]They didn't even notice he was there.

[154:22]He said that he left the group chat
